sections.heading: A Government Without a Playbook

content.simplified:
On April 30, 1789, George Washington became the first president of the United States. He was so nervous that his hands shook during his speech!

Washington had a big problem. The Constitution told him he was president, but it didn't explain how to be president. There were no rules about what to call him, who would help him, or how to handle everyday problems. Everything he did would be the first time anyone had ever done it.

Because he was first, every choice Washington made became an example for future presidents. We call these examples precedents. It's like being the first kid at a new school — whatever you do, everyone after you will copy.
content.standard:
On April 30, 1789, George Washington stood on a balcony in New York City and took the oath of office as the first President of the United States. His hands trembled as he read his inaugural address, and some in the audience could barely hear him. The general who had led armies across battlefields was nervous — because he knew that every single thing he did would set a precedent for every president who came after him.

The Constitution created the job of president, but it was remarkably vague about the details. How should people address the president? What advisors should he have? How much power should he really use? None of this was written down. Washington had to figure it all out from scratch.

Vice President John Adams suggested the president be called "His Highness" or "His Excellency," like European kings. Others thought that sounded too royal for a democracy. Congress even joked about calling the rather plump Adams "His Rotundity." Washington settled on the simple title we still use today: "Mr. President."
content.advanced:
On April 30, 1789, George Washington took the oath of office at Federal Hall in New York City, inaugurating not just a presidency but an experiment in republican governance unprecedented in the modern world. His hands reportedly trembled as he delivered his address — a telling detail for a man who had stared down the British army without flinching. Washington understood something his audience may not have fully grasped: every decision he made, no matter how small, would become a precedent that would shape the office for generations.

The Constitution's Article II was deliberately vague about presidential power. It established the office but left enormous questions unanswered: What was the proper relationship between the president and Congress? How much independence should executive departments have? Could the president remove officials without Senate approval? These weren't abstract debates — they would determine whether the new government functioned as a true republic or drifted toward monarchy.

Even the question of how to address the president carried political weight. Vice President Adams's proposal for royal-sounding titles like "His Highness" reflected Federalist assumptions about the dignity of executive power. Anti-Federalists saw this as dangerous. Washington's choice of the simple "Mr. President" was itself a political statement — signaling that in America, the leader served the people, not the other way around.

sections.keyIdea: Washington's early decisions became precedents — traditions and examples that future presidents would follow. Even small choices, like what to call the president, shaped how American democracy would work.

checkIn.prompt: Why do you think Washington was so nervous about being the first president? Can you think of a time when you were the first person to do something and everyone was watching?
checkIn.hint: Think about what it feels like when everyone copies what you do...

sections.sourceNote: Adapted from American Yawp MS Ch. 6 and TCI Ch. 11, Section 1

sections.heading: Building the Cabinet

content.simplified:
Washington knew he couldn't run the whole government by himself. He needed helpers. So Congress created three departments to handle the biggest jobs: the Department of State (dealing with other countries), the Department of Treasury (handling money), and the Department of War (protecting the country).

Washington picked people he trusted to lead each one. He chose Thomas Jefferson for State, Alexander Hamilton for Treasury, and Henry Knox for War. These leaders became known as the president's Cabinet — his closest group of advisors.

Washington met with his Cabinet often to ask for their ideas and advice. This was another precedent — every president since then has had a Cabinet too.
content.standard:
Congress quickly created three executive departments to help the president govern: the Department of State to handle relations with other countries, the Department of the Treasury to manage the nation's money, and the Department of War to oversee the military. Congress also created the position of Attorney General as the president's legal advisor.

Washington chose men he trusted to lead each one. For Secretary of State, he picked Thomas Jefferson, the brilliant author of the Declaration of Independence. For Secretary of the Treasury, he chose Alexander Hamilton, his ambitious former aide-de-camp from the Revolution. For Secretary of War, he selected Henry Knox, a Boston bookseller who had become one of Washington's most reliable generals.

These department heads became known as the president's Cabinet — a group of advisors who met regularly with the president to discuss policy. The Cabinet isn't mentioned anywhere in the Constitution. Washington simply started doing it, and every president since has followed his example.

Washington chose Hamilton and Jefferson because of their talent. But as you'll see, putting these two strong-willed, deeply different men in the same room was like lighting a fuse.
content.advanced:
The first Congress moved quickly to build the infrastructure of governance, establishing three executive departments: State, Treasury, and War, along with the position of Attorney General. These decisions — seemingly administrative — carried enormous constitutional weight. By creating departments answerable to the president, Congress was implicitly accepting a strong executive model rather than the legislative supremacy some Anti-Federalists had envisioned.

Washington's appointments revealed his priorities. Thomas Jefferson, the polymath who had penned the Declaration of Independence and served as minister to France, would manage foreign affairs. Alexander Hamilton, the Caribbean-born prodigy who had served as Washington's chief aide during the Revolution, would tackle the nation's precarious finances. Henry Knox, the artillery commander who had dragged cannons from Fort Ticonderoga to break the siege of Boston, would oversee military matters. Edmund Randolph, Virginia's former governor, became Attorney General.

Washington's practice of meeting regularly with these department heads established the Cabinet system — a convention found nowhere in the Constitution but central to every subsequent administration. More significantly, Washington treated his Cabinet as genuine advisors rather than mere administrators, creating a deliberative model of executive decision-making. This approach would be severely tested when Hamilton and Jefferson's competing visions for the nation turned Cabinet meetings into ideological battlegrounds.

sections.keyIdea: Washington created the Cabinet system — a group of trusted advisors who help the president make decisions. This wasn't in the Constitution; it was a precedent Washington established that every president has followed since.

checkIn.question: Which of the following is true about Washington's Cabinet?
options: It was specifically described in the Constitution
It was a precedent Washington established on his own
It only included two members
Congress voted against creating it
checkIn.explanation: The Cabinet isn't mentioned in the Constitution. Washington created the practice of meeting regularly with his department heads, and every president since has followed this precedent.

sections.sourceNote: Adapted from TCI Ch. 11, Sections 1-2 and American Yawp MS Ch. 6

sections.heading: Hamilton's Big Bet

content.simplified:
The new country had a big money problem. During the Revolutionary War, the government had borrowed millions of dollars. Now it owed all that money back, and the treasury was basically empty.

Alexander Hamilton had a bold plan with three parts. First, the federal government would pay back ALL the war debts — even the debts that individual states owed. Second, he wanted to create a national bank where the government could keep its money safely and make loans. Third, he wanted to put a tariff (a tax on goods from other countries) to raise money and help American businesses.

Many people were angry about the debt plan. Southern states had already paid their debts. Why should they help pay for northern states that hadn't? Hamilton made a deal: he would support moving the nation's capital to a southern location (which became Washington, D.C.) if southerners would support his debt plan.
content.standard:
When Hamilton took over the Treasury, he found a nation drowning in debt. The federal government owed about $54 million from the Revolutionary War, and the states owed another $25 million. Foreign countries were starting to wonder if America would ever pay them back. Hamilton knew that if the country couldn't manage its money, no one would trust or respect it.

Hamilton's plan was bold and controversial. First, the federal government would assume (take over) all the states' war debts. This would bind wealthy creditors to the national government — if the government owed you money, you'd want it to succeed. Second, he proposed a national bank — the Bank of the United States — to hold government funds, print stable paper currency, and make loans to businesses. Third, he pushed for tariffs on imported goods to raise revenue and protect young American industries from foreign competition.

Southern states erupted. Virginia, Maryland, and others had already paid off their war debts. Why should their taxpayers help bail out Massachusetts and New York? To save his plan, Hamilton struck a deal over dinner with Jefferson and Madison: he'd support moving the permanent capital to a location on the Potomac River (which became Washington, D.C.) if they'd round up enough southern votes for his debt plan. The deal worked — barely.
content.advanced:
Hamilton inherited a fiscal crisis that threatened the republic's survival. The federal government carried approximately $54 million in debt, while the states collectively owed another $25 million. America's credit in European capitals was virtually nonexistent. Hamilton understood that financial credibility was inseparable from political legitimacy — a nation that couldn't manage its debts couldn't command respect on the world stage.

His "Report on Public Credit" (1790) proposed a comprehensive financial system. Federal assumption of state debts would accomplish multiple goals simultaneously: it would establish national creditworthiness, create a class of creditors personally invested in the federal government's success, and shift financial power from the states to the national level. The proposed Bank of the United States would provide institutional infrastructure — a stable currency, a repository for federal funds, and a mechanism for extending credit to commercial enterprises. Protective tariffs would generate revenue while nurturing America's infant manufacturing sector.

The opposition was fierce and regionally charged. Southern states that had retired their debts saw assumption as a wealth transfer to the North. The issue nearly deadlocked the government until the famous dinner at Jefferson's residence, where Hamilton, Jefferson, and Madison brokered a compromise: southern support for assumption in exchange for locating the permanent capital on the Potomac. This "Compromise of 1790" illustrates how early American governance depended on political horse-trading as much as constitutional principle — a dynamic that persists today.

sections.keyIdea: Hamilton's financial plan had three parts: pay all war debts (federal and state), create a national bank, and impose tariffs. It was controversial because it helped some states more than others and expanded federal power.

checkIn.question: What were the three main parts of Hamilton's financial plan?
options: Pay war debts, create a national bank, impose tariffs
Lower taxes, shrink the army, build roads
Print more money, sell western land, raise tariffs
End trade with Britain, ally with France, tax farmers
checkIn.explanation: Hamilton wanted to pay off all war debts (including states' debts), establish the Bank of the United States, and use tariffs to raise revenue and protect American businesses.

sections.sourceNote: Adapted from TCI Ch. 11, Sections 3-4 and American Yawp MS Ch. 6

sections.heading: The Great Debate: Hamilton vs. Jefferson

content.simplified:
Hamilton and Jefferson disagreed about almost everything. It wasn't just about the bank — it was about what kind of country America should be.

Hamilton wanted a strong national government that helped businesses grow. He thought America's future was in cities, factories, and trade. He believed in loose construction — the idea that the Constitution allowed the government to do things it didn't specifically forbid.

Jefferson wanted a smaller government that mostly left people alone. He thought America's future was with farmers and small towns. He believed in strict construction — the idea that the government could ONLY do exactly what the Constitution said.

Their biggest fight was over the national bank. Jefferson said the Constitution never mentions a bank, so Congress can't create one. Hamilton said the Constitution gives Congress the power to do what's "necessary and proper," and a bank is necessary. Washington sided with Hamilton.
content.standard:
The fight over the bank revealed something deeper: Hamilton and Jefferson had completely different visions for America's future. These weren't just policy disagreements — they were arguments about the soul of the country.

Hamilton envisioned a commercial nation. He saw America's destiny in bustling cities, manufacturing, and global trade. He believed ordinary people were driven by self-interest and needed strong leadership from educated elites. He supported loose construction of the Constitution — the idea that the "necessary and proper" clause gave Congress broad powers beyond what was explicitly listed.

Jefferson dreamed of an agrarian republic. He believed independent farmers were the backbone of democracy — people who worked the land, owed nothing to anyone, and could think for themselves. He saw cities as breeding grounds for corruption and poverty. He insisted on strict construction — if the Constitution didn't specifically grant a power, the government didn't have it.

Their personal styles matched their politics. Hamilton was a sharp dresser who thrived in the energy of New York City. Jefferson was a casual Virginia planter who carried a violin on his travels. Hamilton moved fast, from task to task. Jefferson was a thinker who took his time exploring ideas. But both were brilliant, and both believed they were saving America from the other one's terrible ideas.

Washington listened to both sides on the bank question. Jefferson and Attorney General Edmund Randolph said it was unconstitutional. Hamilton wrote a passionate defense arguing the Constitution's elastic clause gave Congress implied powers. Washington sided with Hamilton. The Bank of the United States was established in 1791.
content.advanced:
The constitutional debate over the bank exposed the fundamental ideological fault line of the early republic. At stake was not merely a financial institution but the very nature of federal power and the kind of society America would become.

Hamilton's vision was broadly commercial and nationalist. Drawing on his wartime experience with the fragile Continental Congress, he believed that only a vigorous central government could maintain order, attract investment, and compete with European powers. His loose constructionism rested on the Constitution's "necessary and proper" clause (Article I, Section 8), which he argued granted Congress implied powers to carry out its enumerated duties. If Congress could collect taxes and regulate commerce, it could logically create a bank to facilitate those functions.

Jefferson's counter-vision was agrarian and decentralist. Influenced by Enlightenment ideals and his experience in France, he believed republican virtue depended on an independent citizenry of yeoman farmers, free from the corrupting influence of urban commerce and concentrated wealth. His strict constructionism held that powers not explicitly delegated to the federal government were reserved to the states and the people — a reading that would later find expression in the Tenth Amendment.

The debate also reflected divergent views of human nature. Hamilton's famous assertion that "every man ought to be supposed a knave" justified strong institutional checks. Jefferson's faith in the "good sense" of informed citizens supported broader popular participation. These competing assumptions about human nature and government power have animated American political debate from the 1790s to the present.

Washington's decision to sign the bank bill was arguably the most consequential precedent of his presidency. By accepting Hamilton's implied-powers argument, he established a pattern of expansive constitutional interpretation that would shape federal authority for centuries.

sections.keyIdea: Hamilton and Jefferson had completely different visions for America. Hamilton wanted a strong government, cities, and industry. Jefferson wanted limited government, farming, and individual freedom. Their disagreement created the foundation for America's first political parties.

checkIn.prompt: Hamilton thought ordinary people were too selfish to govern themselves wisely. Jefferson thought farmers had natural good sense. Who do you think was closer to right — and why?
checkIn.hint: Think about people you know. Are most people mainly looking out for themselves, or do they try to do the right thing?

sections.sourceNote: Adapted from TCI Ch. 11, Sections 3-4 and American Yawp MS Ch. 6

sections.heading: The Whiskey Rebellion

content.simplified:
To help pay off the country's debts, Hamilton convinced Congress to put a tax on whiskey. This made frontier farmers furious.

Why whiskey? Farmers in western Pennsylvania grew lots of grain, but it was too heavy and bulky to carry over the mountains to sell in eastern cities. So they turned their grain into whiskey, which was smaller, lighter, and worth more money. For these farmers, whiskey was basically their currency — they even used it to pay for things at the store.

When the tax collectors showed up, the farmers fought back. They tarred and feathered tax collectors and burned their houses. By 1794, about 7,000 angry farmers had gathered to resist.

President Washington was not going to let this slide. He personally led 13,000 soldiers to put down the rebellion. When the army showed up, the rebels went home without a fight. Washington had made an important point: the new government was strong enough to enforce its laws.
content.standard:
Hamilton's financial plan included a tax on whiskey — and for farmers on the western frontier, that felt like a direct attack on their lives. Grain was heavy and spoiled quickly, making it nearly impossible to transport over the Appalachian Mountains to eastern markets. But turn that grain into whiskey, and you had something compact, valuable, and easy to trade. Whiskey was literally currency on the frontier.

In 1791, tax collectors in western Pennsylvania were met with rage. Protesters tarred and feathered one collector and burned another's house. By 1794, about seven thousand armed farmers had organized, threatening federal officials and shutting down any attempt to collect the tax.

Washington and Hamilton saw this as a direct threat to the government's authority. If people could simply refuse to obey laws they didn't like, the Constitution was meaningless. Washington personally led 13,000 militia troops into western Pennsylvania — the only time a sitting president has ever led troops in the field. The rebels scattered without a real fight.

Jefferson saw it differently. He thought sending an army to crush a few farmers was exactly the kind of tyranny the Revolution had been fought to prevent. He believed Hamilton was using the rebellion as an excuse to flex government muscle and terrify anyone who disagreed with Federalist policies.

Both sides had a point. The rebellion showed the new government could enforce its laws — but it also raised uncomfortable questions about when enforcement becomes oppression.
content.advanced:
The Whiskey Rebellion of 1794 was the first major test of federal authority under the Constitution and a defining moment for how the new government would balance order with liberty. The excise tax on distilled spirits, a cornerstone of Hamilton's revenue program, fell disproportionately on western farmers for whom whiskey production was not merely an economic activity but the primary means of converting surplus grain into a transportable, tradeable commodity.

The resistance escalated from individual acts of defiance — tarring and feathering tax collectors, burning their property — to organized armed opposition involving approximately seven thousand participants. The rebels explicitly invoked the rhetoric of the American Revolution, arguing that taxation without adequate representation (western districts were underrepresented in state and federal government) justified resistance.

Washington's decision to mobilize 13,000 militia troops — a force larger than most Continental Army deployments — was calibrated for political as much as military effect. Hamilton accompanied the expedition, eager to demonstrate federal power. The overwhelming show of force succeeded in dispersing the rebellion without significant bloodshed, but it also established a troubling precedent: the willingness of the federal government to use military force against its own citizens over tax disputes.

The episode illuminated the tension between two competing revolutionary legacies. Federalists argued that legitimate resistance required working through constitutional channels — elections, petitions, courts. Republicans countered that the right of the people to resist unjust laws was foundational to American identity. This unresolved tension between lawful authority and popular resistance would resurface throughout American history, from the Nullification Crisis to the Civil Rights Movement.

sections.keyIdea: The Whiskey Rebellion tested whether the new government could enforce its own laws. Washington's response proved it could — but Jefferson worried that crushing protesters with an army was going too far.

checkIn.prompt: The Whiskey Rebels said they were doing the same thing the American revolutionaries had done — resisting unfair taxes. Washington said they were breaking the law. Who was right?
checkIn.hint: What's the difference between the colonists resisting British taxes and citizens resisting taxes from their own elected government?

sections.sourceNote: Adapted from TCI Ch. 11, Section 2 and American Yawp MS Ch. 6

sections.heading: Staying Out of It: Washington's Foreign Policy

content.simplified:
While Americans argued about money and taxes, trouble was brewing across the ocean. In 1789, the French people had their own revolution, overthrowing their king. At first, most Americans cheered — it felt like France was copying what America had done!

But the French Revolution turned violent. The revolutionaries started cutting off people's heads with a machine called a guillotine. Thousands died. Then France went to war with Britain, and both countries started causing problems for American ships.

Washington decided America should stay out of it. The country was too young and too weak to fight another war. He issued a Neutrality Proclamation, declaring that America would not take sides.

But staying neutral was hard. Britain was stopping American ships and kidnapping American sailors. Washington sent John Jay to Britain to negotiate. Jay's Treaty wasn't great — Britain agreed to leave some forts but kept kidnapping sailors — but it kept America out of war. Many people were furious about it anyway.
content.standard:
When the French Revolution erupted in 1789, most Americans cheered. France was following America's example! Towns held parades and people called each other "Citizen" in the French fashion. Jefferson's supporters saw it as proof that liberty was spreading across the world.

Then the guillotine started falling. The French revolutionary government executed King Louis XVI, Queen Marie Antoinette, and then thousands of its own citizens during the Reign of Terror. The revolution that had begun with hope descended into bloodshed. Americans who had cheered suddenly had to decide: Was this still liberty, or had it become madness?

The answer split the country along party lines. Jefferson's Democratic-Republicans continued to support France, arguing that revolution was always messy. Hamilton's Federalists backed Britain, horrified by the violence. When France and Britain went to war, both sides began seizing American merchant ships and cargo.

Washington declared American neutrality — the nation would not take sides. But neutrality was easier to declare than to maintain. British warships were stopping American vessels and using impressment — kidnapping American sailors and forcing them to serve in the Royal Navy.

Washington sent Chief Justice John Jay to negotiate with Britain. Jay's Treaty (1794) got the British to abandon frontier forts they still occupied and pay for some shipping losses. But it failed to stop impressment and committed the U.S. to favorable trade terms with Britain. People were furious — they burned effigies of Jay in the streets. But the treaty kept America out of a war it wasn't ready to fight.
content.advanced:
The French Revolution's descent from constitutional reform to the Reign of Terror created the first major foreign policy crisis of the new republic and deepened the domestic partisan divide. Jefferson and his allies interpreted the revolution through the lens of 1776 — messy, violent, but fundamentally a struggle for liberty against tyranny. Hamilton and the Federalists, many of whom had commercial ties to Britain and feared social upheaval, recoiled from the violence and saw dangerous parallels to mob rule at home.

Washington's Neutrality Proclamation of 1793 established a critical precedent: the president could unilaterally determine foreign policy without congressional approval. This assertion of executive authority in foreign affairs — not explicitly granted by the Constitution — remains a cornerstone of presidential power today.

The practical challenges of neutrality were severe. Both Britain and France were seizing American merchant vessels. British impressment of American sailors — an estimated 10,000 between 1793 and 1812 — was particularly galling, as it violated American sovereignty at its most fundamental level. Jay's Treaty represented a pragmatic calculation: the United States lacked the naval power to challenge Britain and risked economic catastrophe if trade was disrupted. The treaty's deeply unpopular terms — which conceded most American grievances while securing only limited British withdrawals from frontier posts — revealed the painful reality of a minor power negotiating with a global empire.

Jay's Treaty also had significant domestic consequences. It cemented partisan alignments, with Federalists supporting the British orientation and Democratic-Republicans viewing it as a betrayal of French alliance obligations. Washington's Farewell Address of 1796, warning against "entangling alliances" and partisan faction, was in many ways a response to the divisions these foreign policy debates had created.

sections.keyIdea: Washington tried to keep America neutral in the war between France and Britain. Jay's Treaty avoided war but was deeply unpopular. Washington's Farewell Address warned future leaders to avoid getting pulled into foreign conflicts.

checkIn.question: Why was Jay's Treaty so unpopular with many Americans?
options: It declared war on France
It failed to stop British impressment of American sailors
It gave Britain control of American ports
It raised taxes on American farmers
checkIn.explanation: Jay's Treaty got some concessions from Britain, but it failed to address the biggest issue — British ships were still kidnapping American sailors (impressment). Many Americans felt the treaty gave away too much.

sections.sourceNote: Adapted from TCI Ch. 11 and American Yawp MS Ch. 6

sections.heading: Adams Takes Over

content.simplified:
When Washington retired after two terms (another important precedent!), John Adams became the second president. Adams had a tough job. France was angry that America had signed Jay's Treaty with Britain, and French ships started attacking American merchant vessels.

Adams sent diplomats to France to work things out. But when they arrived, three French officials (nicknamed X, Y, and Z) demanded a huge bribe before they would even talk. Americans were outraged. "Millions for defense, but not one cent for tribute!" people shouted. This became known as the XYZ Affair.

Adams managed to avoid going to war with France, which was the right decision. But his time as president was rocky, and many people were unhappy with him by the time the next election came around.
content.standard:
Washington's decision to step down after two terms was perhaps his greatest precedent of all. He could have been president for life — many people wanted him to be. Instead, he voluntarily gave up power, proving that in America, no one was bigger than the republic. His Farewell Address warned against political parties and foreign entanglements — advice the country would immediately ignore.

John Adams, a Federalist, won the presidency in 1796 in a bitter election against Jefferson. Under the original electoral system, Jefferson — the runner-up — became Adams's vice president. Imagine a president and vice president from opposing parties who basically despise each other's politics. That was Adams and Jefferson.

Adams inherited Washington's foreign policy headaches. France, furious about Jay's Treaty, began seizing American ships. When Adams sent diplomats to negotiate, three French agents (dubbed "X," "Y," and "Z") demanded a $250,000 bribe and a $10 million loan before they'd even start talking. When this became public, Americans were outraged. The cry went up: "Millions for defense, but not one cent for tribute!"

The XYZ Affair pushed the country to the brink of war. Congress created a navy, and an undeclared naval conflict with France (called the "Quasi-War") broke out. But Adams, to his credit, chose diplomacy over full-scale war. He sent new negotiators to France, and a peace agreement was reached in 1800. It may have cost him re-election — War Hawks in his own party were furious — but Adams always considered it his finest achievement.
content.advanced:
Washington's voluntary retirement after two terms — a precedent that held until Franklin Roosevelt and was later codified in the Twenty-Second Amendment — was a revolutionary act in itself. In an era when leaders clung to power, Washington's willingness to relinquish it demonstrated that republican principles could triumph over personal ambition. His Farewell Address, largely drafted by Hamilton, articulated two enduring warnings: against the "baneful effects of the spirit of party" and against "permanent alliances" with foreign nations.

Adams's presidency was defined by the crisis with France. The XYZ Affair transformed public opinion overnight, generating a wave of anti-French sentiment that Federalists exploited to expand military spending and pass the Alien and Sedition Acts. The Sedition Act — which criminalized "false, scandalous, and malicious" criticism of the government — represented perhaps the most direct assault on First Amendment freedoms in American history. Federalist prosecutors used it to jail Republican newspaper editors, effectively criminalizing political opposition.

Adams's decision to pursue peace with France over the objections of the Hamiltonian wing of his own party was an act of considerable political courage. The Convention of 1800 ended the Quasi-War and released the United States from its 1778 alliance with France, but it split the Federalist Party irreparably. Adams later reflected that he wished his tombstone to read: "Here lies John Adams, who took upon himself the responsibility of the peace with France in the year 1800." His prioritization of national interest over partisan advantage stands as one of the most principled presidential decisions of the era.

sections.keyIdea: Adams faced a crisis with France (the XYZ Affair) and chose peace over war, even though it was politically unpopular. Washington's decision to retire after two terms set a precedent that lasted until the 1940s.

checkIn.prompt: Adams chose peace with France even though it probably cost him the next election. Was that brave leadership or bad politics? Can it be both?
checkIn.hint: Think about whether doing the right thing and doing the popular thing are always the same...

sections.sourceNote: Adapted from TCI Ch. 11, Section 5 and American Yawp MS Ch. 6

sections.heading: Segment 1 Summary: Washington's First Decisions

content.simplified:
Here's what you need to remember from this segment:

George Washington was the first president, and everything he did set an example (precedent) for future presidents. He created the Cabinet, chose the title "Mr. President," and stepped down after two terms.

Alexander Hamilton created a financial plan to pay off war debts, start a national bank, and raise money through tariffs. Thomas Jefferson disagreed with almost all of it.

The Whiskey Rebellion showed the new government was strong enough to enforce its laws. Jay's Treaty kept America out of war with Britain but was very unpopular.

John Adams became the second president and handled the XYZ Affair with France. He chose peace over war.
content.standard:
The first years under the Constitution established the patterns that would define American governance. Washington's precedents — the Cabinet, the two-term limit, presidential neutrality, the title "Mr. President" — gave shape to an office the Constitution had left deliberately vague.

Hamilton's financial plan (debt assumption, the national bank, tariffs) strengthened the federal government but ignited the first great ideological battle of the republic: Hamilton's vision of a commercial, industrial nation vs. Jefferson's dream of an agrarian republic. Their argument over strict vs. loose construction of the Constitution is still being debated today.

The Whiskey Rebellion proved the new government could enforce its laws — unlike the old Articles of Confederation. Jay's Treaty and the Neutrality Proclamation kept America out of the war between France and Britain, even though neutrality pleased almost nobody.

Adams navigated the XYZ Affair and chose diplomacy over war with France, demonstrating that principled leadership sometimes means making unpopular decisions. Washington's Farewell Address warned against both partisan division and foreign entanglements — warnings that seem more relevant than ever.
content.advanced:
Segment 1 covers the critical period from 1789 to 1797 when constitutional text was translated into governing practice. The key analytical themes are:

Precedent as power: Washington's decisions about executive conduct, Cabinet governance, neutrality, and voluntary retirement didn't just establish traditions — they created constitutional norms with the force of law. The presidency was shaped as much by Washington's choices as by Article II.

The Hamilton-Jefferson divide: Their disagreement over the bank crystallized competing interpretations of federal power that persist in modern constitutional law. Hamilton's loose constructionism (emphasizing implied powers and the "necessary and proper" clause) vs. Jefferson's strict constructionism (emphasizing enumerated powers and the Tenth Amendment) remains the central framework for debates about government authority.

The limits of neutrality: Washington's foreign policy demonstrated both the appeal and the difficulty of non-alignment for a minor power in a world dominated by great-power rivalry. Jay's Treaty illustrated the painful trade-offs weak nations face when negotiating with stronger ones.

The tension between order and liberty: From the Whiskey Rebellion to Adams's handling of the Quasi-War, the early republic repeatedly confronted the question of how much authority a democratic government can exercise before it becomes the tyranny it was designed to prevent.

sections.keyIdea: The first years of the republic established the precedents, debates, and tensions that would shape American government for centuries. The arguments between Hamilton and Jefferson about federal power are still going on today.

checkIn.question: Which of these was NOT a precedent set by Washington?
options: Creating the Cabinet
Serving only two terms as president
Establishing the Supreme Court's power of judicial review
Using the title 'Mr. President'
checkIn.explanation: Judicial review was established by Chief Justice John Marshall in Marbury v. Madison (1803), during Jefferson's presidency — not by Washington. Washington's precedents included the Cabinet, the two-term tradition, and the simple title 'Mr. President.'

sections.sourceNote: Summary of Segment 1 content

sections.heading: How Disagreements Became Parties

content.simplified:
Remember how Hamilton and Jefferson fought about the bank, the economy, and how strong the government should be? Those fights didn't stay private. Soon, people across the country started picking sides.

People who agreed with Hamilton called themselves Federalists. They wanted a strong national government and supported business and trade. People who agreed with Jefferson called themselves Democratic-Republicans (or just Republicans). They wanted a smaller government and supported farmers.

These were America's first political parties — organized groups of people who share similar ideas about how the government should work. Washington hated the idea of parties. He warned that they would tear the country apart. But the disagreements were too big to ignore.
content.standard:
The Constitution says nothing about political parties. In fact, most of the Founders — especially Washington — dreaded the idea. They called them "factions" and warned that organized political groups would put party loyalty above national interest.

But the Hamilton-Jefferson divide was too fundamental to stay contained in Cabinet meetings. As their disagreements spilled into newspapers, taverns, and state legislatures, supporters on each side began to organize. Hamilton's allies formed the Federalist Party. Jefferson and Madison built the Democratic-Republican Party (often just called Republicans — not the same as today's Republican Party).

Each party developed its own network of newspapers, local organizers, and loyal voters. Federalist papers attacked Jefferson as a dangerous radical who would unleash French-style chaos. Republican papers painted Hamilton as a secret monarchist who wanted to crown himself king. The insults were vicious, personal, and often completely made up.

Washington watched this with despair. In his Farewell Address, he warned that the "spirit of party" was democracy's worst enemy — that it would "agitate the community with ill-founded jealousies and false alarms" and eventually open the door to tyranny. But by the time he left office, the two-party system was already a fact of American life.
content.advanced:
The emergence of organized political parties in the 1790s was one of the most consequential — and least anticipated — developments of the early republic. The Constitution's framers, steeped in classical republican theory, viewed "faction" as a disease of democratic government. Madison himself had written in Federalist No. 10 that controlling faction was the central challenge of republican governance. Yet within a decade, he and Jefferson were building exactly the kind of organized political opposition the framers had feared.

The transition from Cabinet disagreement to partisan organization followed a recognizable pattern: ideological conflict → newspaper warfare → grassroots mobilization → electoral competition. Hamilton used the Gazette of the United States to promote Federalist views; Jefferson and Madison countered by secretly funding Philip Freneau's National Gazette. These partisan newspapers didn't pretend to be objective — they were weapons in an ideological war.

What makes this development historically significant is that the early republic accidentally invented something no political theorist had planned for: a system of legitimate opposition. The idea that a "loyal opposition" could criticize the government without being treasonous was genuinely revolutionary. The Federalists' Sedition Act showed how fragile this concept was — they literally tried to criminalize political opposition. The fact that the Democratic-Republicans survived, won power in 1800, and then (mostly) didn't jail their opponents in return was a crucial test of democratic norms.

sections.keyIdea: America's first political parties — Federalists and Democratic-Republicans — grew out of the Hamilton-Jefferson disagreements. Washington warned against parties, but the divisions were too deep to prevent them.

checkIn.prompt: Washington warned that political parties would tear the country apart. Do you think he was right? What are the good and bad sides of having political parties?
checkIn.hint: Think about what would happen if there were no parties at all — would that be better or worse?

sections.sourceNote: Adapted from TCI Ch. 11, Sections 3-5 and American Yawp MS Ch. 6

sections.heading: Federalists vs. Democratic-Republicans

content.simplified:
The two parties disagreed about almost everything. Here are the big differences:

Government power: Federalists wanted a strong national government. Democratic-Republicans wanted the states to have more power.

The Constitution: Federalists believed in loose construction — the government can do things the Constitution doesn't specifically forbid. Democratic-Republicans believed in strict construction — the government can ONLY do what the Constitution specifically says.

The economy: Federalists supported banks, factories, and trade. Democratic-Republicans supported farming.

Foreign policy: Federalists favored Britain. Democratic-Republicans favored France.

The military: Federalists wanted a standing army (a permanent army that's always ready). Democratic-Republicans thought a permanent army was dangerous and preferred calling up citizen militias only when needed.

Who supported them: Federalists were popular with wealthy merchants and businesspeople in the North. Democratic-Republicans were popular with farmers, workers, and people in the South and West.
content.standard:
The two parties represented fundamentally different answers to the same question: What kind of country should America be? Here's how they compared across the key issues:

Government power: Federalists believed a strong central government was essential to hold the nation together and promote economic growth. Democratic-Republicans feared that a powerful government would trample individual rights and state sovereignty, becoming no better than the British monarchy.

Constitutional interpretation: This was the heart of the divide. Federalists supported loose construction — the Constitution's "necessary and proper" clause gave Congress broad implied powers. Democratic-Republicans demanded strict construction — if a power wasn't explicitly listed in the Constitution, the government didn't have it.

Economy: Federalists envisioned America as a commercial and industrial nation, with cities, banks, and global trade. Democratic-Republicans saw America's future in agriculture — independent farmers who owed nothing to anyone were the ideal citizens of a republic.

Foreign alliances: Federalists leaned toward Britain (a stable monarchy with a strong economy). Democratic-Republicans sympathized with France (a fellow republic fighting for liberty).

Military: Federalists wanted a standing army — a permanent professional military. Democratic-Republicans saw standing armies as tools of tyranny and preferred citizen militias called up only in emergencies.

Supporters: Federalist strength was concentrated among merchants, lawyers, and clergy in New England and the mid-Atlantic. Democratic-Republicans drew support from farmers, artisans, and frontier settlers, especially in the South and West.
content.advanced:
The Federalist-Republican divide mapped onto nearly every dimension of political life, creating what historians recognize as the "First Party System." Understanding these alignments requires seeing them not as arbitrary preferences but as internally coherent worldviews rooted in different social experiences and philosophical traditions.

Constitutional philosophy: The strict vs. loose construction debate was ultimately about the nature of delegated power. Federalists, drawing on British common law tradition, argued that a government needed flexibility to address unforeseen challenges. Republicans, influenced by Enlightenment social contract theory, insisted that sovereignty resided with the people, who had delegated only specific, enumerated powers. This tension — between governmental effectiveness and individual liberty — remains the central axis of American constitutional law.

Political economy: The economic divide reflected genuinely different material interests. Northern merchants and financiers stood to gain from Hamilton's commercial system; southern planters and western farmers feared it would subordinate their interests to urban capital. But the divide was also ideological: Hamilton's political economy assumed that concentrated wealth and expertise were prerequisites for national development, while Jefferson's agrarianism held that widely distributed property ownership was the foundation of republican virtue.

Geopolitics and identity: The British vs. French orientation reflected competing visions of what America represented. Federalists saw the British constitutional tradition as America's natural heritage. Republicans saw the French Revolution as an extension of 1776. These foreign policy preferences were inseparable from domestic identity — to be "pro-French" or "pro-British" was to declare what kind of American you were.

Regional consolidation: The geographic concentration of party support — Federalists in the commercial Northeast, Republicans in the agrarian South and West — foreshadowed the sectionalism that would eventually produce the Civil War. The First Party System was, in many ways, a rehearsal for sectional conflict.

sections.keyIdea: The two parties disagreed on nearly everything: how to read the Constitution, what the economy should look like, which foreign country to support, and whether to have a permanent army. These weren't just political differences — they were competing visions of America's future.

checkIn.question: A politician says: 'The Constitution doesn't specifically give Congress the power to create a space program, so Congress can't do it.' This is an example of:
options: Loose construction
Strict construction
Judicial review
A precedent
checkIn.explanation: Strict construction means the government can ONLY do what the Constitution specifically says. A strict constructionist would argue that if a power isn't listed, the government doesn't have it. A loose constructionist would counter that the 'necessary and proper' clause allows Congress to adapt.
